pattern: Without a BST's ordering to steer you, the split point between two targets can only be found by asking each subtree whether it contains either target and merging the answers on the way back up.
hints: There is no value comparison that tells you which side to go — the tree has no ordering guarantee here.
Recurse into both children; a node knows it is the split point once both sides report finding a target (or it is a target itself).
If exactly one side reports a find, pass that result upward unchanged; the first node where both sides agree is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: targets split across the root's two subtrees, values not in BST order
one target is an ancestor of the other; answer is that ancestor itself
left child larger than root and right child smaller — breaks any BST-direction assumption
smallest possible tree, parent and child
both targets are the root's direct children
right-skewed unsorted chain, targets deep and adjacent

stress.description: randomly shaped tree (unrelated to value order) with two distinct target values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 140))
    vals = rng.sample(range(-10000, 10001), n)
    def build(items):
        if not items:
            return None
        if len(items) == 1:
            return [items[0], None, None]
        i = rng.randrange(len(items))
        root_val = items[i]
        rest = items[:i] + items[i + 1:]
        rng.shuffle(rest)
        split = rng.randint(0, len(rest))
        return [root_val, build(rest[:split]), build(rest[split:])]
    tree = build(vals)
    out = []
    queue = [tree]
    while queue:
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if node is None:
            out.append(None)
            continue
        out.append(node[0])
        queue.append(node[1])
        queue.append(node[2])
    while out and out[-1] is None:
        out.pop()
    p, q = rng.sample(vals, 2)
    return [out, p, q]
